title: Dog Chemical Burn in Eye: Emergency Care & First Aid Guide
meta_desc: A chemical burn in your dog's eye is a medical emergency. Learn immediate first aid steps and why you must contact a vet immediately.
content_html:
What is a Chemical Burn in a Dog's Eye?

A chemical burn occurs when an irritating or corrosive substance—such as household cleaners, fertilizers, or automotive fluids—comes into contact with the sensitive tissues of the eye. These substances can cause rapid damage to the cornea and surrounding tissues.

Is it an Emergency?

Yes. This is a high-level medical emergency. Chemical exposure can cause permanent blindness or the loss of the eye within minutes or hours. You must flush the eye immediately with sterile saline or plain water and contact an emergency veterinarian right away.

How a Photo Helps Triage

While your priority is flushing the eye, sending a photo to your veterinarian or a triage service can help professionals assess the severity of the irritation and the specific area affected. A clear image allows the vet to prepare the necessary treatments before you even arrive at the clinic.

faqs.question: What should I use to flush my dog's eye?
faqs.answer: Use sterile saline solution if available; otherwise, lukewarm tap water is acceptable in an emergency to dilute the chemical.

faqs.question: How long should I flush the eye?
faqs.answer: You should aim to flush the affected eye continuously for at least 15 to 20 minutes before heading to the veterinary clinic.

faqs.question: Can I use human eye drops for the burn?
faqs.answer: No, do not use any medicated human eye drops or ointments unless specifically directed by a veterinarian, as some ingredients can worsen chemical damage.
